elements: rectangular block with horizontal and vertical striped surface
steps-like structure where stripes indicate shading
ambiguous transitional shape between block and steps

interactions.teaches: Rotating the view shows how perception shifts with perspective changes, uncovering multiple interpretations of the shape.
Hovering over different areas emphasizes the distinction between actual paint and shading resulting from depth.

demo_steps.title: Starting Ambiguity
demo_steps.narration: Here, the shape appears as a simple rectangular block with painted stripes. As you begin to rotate, you can see the shape start to transition into something more ambiguous.

demo_steps.title: Block Interpretation
demo_steps.narration: At this angle, the structure looks like a simple block with stripes painted on, suggesting a flat surface without depth variation.

demo_steps.title: Ambiguous Transition
demo_steps.narration: As the view rotates further, the shape becomes quite ambiguous. This midpoint shows the challenge in discerning whether the stripes indicate painted features or changes in depth.

demo_steps.title: Steps Interpretation
demo_steps.narration: Here, the shape is clearly perceived as a set of steps, where the stripes now appear as shading, indicating variations in depth across the steps.

plan.camera_suggestion: Front-facing isometric view that maintains the horizon line of the original image, initially capturing the block perspective and progressively revealing ambiguity.

inline_constraints.explainers: hover tooltips and click popups only
inline_constraints.framing: match the source crop and whitespace; do not force-fill the iframe if the original has margins

plan.notes: Ensure accurate geometric reconstruction with Three.js. Utilize the original color palette to enhance the visual accuracy of the scene.

plan.contextChunk:
Let's run the algorithm with shapes that do not satisfy the assumptions
that we have made for the simple world. @fig-out_of_domain shows the
result when the input image violates several assumptions we made
(shadows are not soft and the green cube occludes the red one) and when
it has one object on top of the other, a configuration that we did not
consider while designing the algorithm. In this case the result happens
to be good, but it could have gone wrong.

![Reconstruction of an out-of-domain example. The input image violates
several assumptions we made (shadows are not soft and the green cube
occludes the red one) and it has one object on top of the other, a
configuration that we did not consider while designing the algorithm.
](figures/simplesystem/out_of_domain_example.png){#fig-out_of_domain
width="100%"}

@fig-impossible shows the *impossible steps* inspired from @Adelson99.
On the left side, this shape looks rectangular and the stripes appear to
be painted on the surface. On the right side, the shape looks as though
it has steps, with the stripes corresponding to shading due to the
surface orientation. In the middle, the shape is ambiguous.
@fig-impossible shows the reconstructed 3D scene for this unlikely
image. The system has tried to approximate the constraints, but for this
shape it is not possible to exactly satisfy all the constraints.

![Reconstruction of an impossible figure (inspired from @Adelson99): the
reconstruction agrees with our 3D
perception.](figures/simplesystem/steps.png){#fig-impossible
width="100%"}

## Concluding Remarks

Despite having a 3D representation of the structure of the scene, the
system is still unaware of the fact that the scene is composed of a set
of distinct objects. For instance, as the system lacks a representation
of which objects are actually present in the scene, we cannot visualize
the occluded parts. The system cannot do simple tasks like counting the
number of cubes.

A different approach to the one discussed here is model-based scene
interpretation, where we could have a set of predefined models of the
objects that can be present in the scene and the system can try to
decide if they are present or not in the image, and recover their

elements: steps-like structure with stripes as shading
an ambiguous transitional shape between block and steps

interactions.teaches: Rotating reveals the multiple interpretations of the shape by changing the apparent perspective.
Hovering over different surfaces highlights them, helping distinguish between painted stripes and shadows.

demo_steps.title: Initial Ambiguity
demo_steps.narration: Initially, the shape appears as a simple block on the left. As you orbit the view, notice how it transitions to the ambiguous middle section.

demo_steps.title: Perception as Painted
demo_steps.narration: From this angle, the stripes seem painted on a rectangular block. The shadows do not suggest any depth changes.

demo_steps.title: Perception as Steps
demo_steps.narration: As you continue to rotate, the stripes form shadows on steps, showing a clear depth change. This demonstrates the power of perspective in altering perception.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Isometric view focusing on the transition between block and steps to highlight ambiguity

inline_constraints.framing: match the source crop and whitespace; do not force-fill the iframe if the original has margins
inline_constraints.camera: infer the source camera angle/projection/zoom so the first rendered frame is a drop-in visual replacement

interactionPlan.notes: Use a dashed line to highlight the transition from stripes to steps without interrupting the perception shift.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The shape in the generated figure looks disorganized compared to the neat stacked structure in the source.
Colors and shading mismatch between the source and generated figure.
The arrangement and alignment of blocks do not reflect the original's clear sections.
The background is white rather than a pale green as in the source image.
evaluation.notes: The generated figure fails to capture the neat structure and color scheme of the source, lacking interaction and accuracy.
action_items: Adjust the block geometry to match the source structure exactly.
Use the same color palette as the source image for both blocks and the background.
Re-align the blocks to reflect the original pattern clearly and improve the camera view to match perspective.

feedback.rationale: The 'Concept-Misunderstood' failure mode and low scores indicate a fundamental issue in understanding the original structure and layout, requiring a new planning phase.

Re-align the blocks to reflect the original pattern clearly and improve the camera view to match perspective.
